intro: Adyen and Checkout.com both show up under the payment gateway category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Adyen uses pricing that starts at EUR 0.11 + scheme fee, while Checkout.com runs on usage-based from custom pricing. Both sit at moderate lock-in. Transparency lands at 3/5 versus 4/5. Adyen fits teams working on enterprise with $10M+ TPV needing global coverage, while Checkout.com is a closer match when the job is SaaS with online payments. Worth noting: Adyen is explicitly not for solo projects; Checkout.com is explicitly not for offline, Internal projects. The honest trade-off: Adyen trades off on startup with <$1M TPV — minimum fees are high; Checkout.com trades off on offline-only retail. On the plus side, Adyen highlights enterprise with $10M+ TPV needing global coverage, while Checkout.com points to SaaS with online payments. Adyen's documentation also calls out unified online + in-store (POS) payments. Checkout.com similarly notes e-commerce checkout. For teams evaluating payment gateway options today, the right choice usually comes down to pricing model, lock-in, and which tool's stated use-case maps to yours.
quick_take: Adyen is for enterprise with $10M+ TPV needing global; Checkout.com is for SaaS with online payments; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Adyen if your project is enterprise with $10M+ TPV needing global coverage, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.
verdict_b: Choose Checkout.com if your project is SaaS with online payments, usage-based billing at custom pricing matches your volume, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.

faq.a: Adyen uses pricing that starts at EUR 0.11 + scheme fee, and Checkout.com uses usage-based from custom pricing. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Adyen to Checkout.com?
faq.a: Our data puts Adyen at medium lock-in (proprietary tokens, but csv import/export under), and Checkout.com at medium lock-in. Migration is feasible but not trivial — budget time for re-integration, data export, and parallel running before cutover.
Both score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis. Checkout.com does edge ahead on pricing/docs transparency (4/5 vs 3/5), which can make evaluation faster.

faq.q: Is Checkout.com a good alternative to Adyen?
faq.a: Checkout.com is a reasonable alternative to Adyen when your workload leans more toward SaaS, E-commerce, Marketplace than enterprise. The pricing model shifts too — Adyen is pricing that starts at EUR 0.11 + scheme fee, Checkout.com is usage-based from custom pricing — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: Checkout.com is explicitly not for offline, Internal projects, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
